figures.caption: Figure 1. KinEMbed architecture and training phases. In Phase I, two MLP encoders (femg, fkin) project EMG features x and kinematic angles y onto a shared 16-dimensional unit hypersphere trained via a contrastive objective with ℓ2-normalisation. In Phase II, the frozen EMG embedding is then passed to a TCN decoder (g) trained via an MSE objective for continuous DoA regression. Full details in Section 3.

Figure 2. Per-DoA R2 for able-bodied subjects. KinEMbed shows the most pronounced gains on thumb rotation and thumb flexion, the most challenging and variable degrees of actuation. ARIMA is omitted due to poor performance (negative R2).
Figure 3. Example 120-second continuous prediction trace for subject 10 (able-bodied). All five DoA are decoded simultaneously from EMG using the frozen femg encoder.

Figure 6. A visualisation of the movements of the hand and wrist (a). Image reproduced from (Cabibihan et al., 2021). The positioning of the joint state sensors (b). Image reproduced from (Krasoulis et al., 2017).
